planting.note: Start indoors early and plant out after frost.

directions: Grow this rose-flowered form exactly like blue hyssop, in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun. Shear lightly after bloom to encourage a second flush of soft pink spikes. Clip the fragrant tops for the pollinator garden or a mild aromatic tea.

planting.note: Raise from seed indoors and transplant near the last frost.

directions: Plant this white-blooming form in gritty, well-drained soil where it forms a tidy little sub-shrub. Prune each spring and again after flowering to keep the growth dense. Harvest the pale flower spikes for bees or dried herbal use as they open in midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in spring or divide established mats.

directions: This showier relative forms a neat mat topped with plump spikes of large purple flowers. Grow it in moist, humus-rich soil and shear spent blooms to tidy the planting and prompt rebloom. Divide clumps every few years in spring to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then start indoors before setting out.

directions: Chill the seed for a few weeks before sowing, since it needs cold to break dormancy. Plant in consistently moist, rich soil at a pond edge or in dappled shade. Harvest the leafy stems dotted with tiny blue flowers when the plant is in full bloom for the calming nervine herb.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant to a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Give this drought-tolerant species full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil, as it resents wet feet. Handsome spikes of purple-blue flowers appear over narrow leaves in summer. The thick yellow roots, harvested from three-year-old plants in fall, are the prized part in traditional medicine.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in damp ground.

directions: Grow this wild mint in consistently moist soil where its running roots can spread, or confine it to a bed. It carries clusters of tiny lilac flowers tucked in the leaf axils rather than in terminal spikes. Cut the strongly menthol-scented stems for the traditional source of natural mint oil.

planting.note: Plant root cuttings in spring; this sterile strain will not seed around.

directions: Bury root cuttings a few inches deep in fertile soil, crown side up. This non-seeding clone stays put, making it the usual choice for a permaculture mulch bank. Cut the leafy growth two to four times a year for a nutrient-rich mulch or liquid feed.

planting.note: A short cold-stratification improves the otherwise slow germination.

directions: Chill the seed briefly, then surface-sow and be patient, as it can be slow to start. Grow in average, well-drained soil in full sun. Harvest the slender flowering spikes in summer as the small pale flowers begin to open.

planting.note: Nick or soak the hard seed coat before sowing to speed germination.

directions: Scarify or soak the hard seed overnight, then sow shallowly in well-drained soil. This legume forms a deep taproot and dislikes soggy ground. Dig the roots in autumn of the third or fourth year, once they have grown long and substantial.

planting.note: Start warm indoors and set out only after frost, as it needs heat and a long season.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and grow the young plants on in heat, as it is frost-tender. In most climates it is raised as an annual in a warm, sunny, well-drained spot. Dig the roots in autumn after the small orange berries form, before the first frost.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring; it makes a rosette the first year and blooms the second.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface and leave it uncovered, as light aids sprouting. It thrives in poor, dry soil and self-sows freely. Fragrant yellow flowers open in the evening of the second summer; collect the ripe seed pods later for oil.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out once nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto warm, moist mix and barely cover it, keeping the surface humid until sprouts appear. Pinch the growing tips often to force a bushy, leafy plant and remove flower spikes if you want continued leaf production. Snip clove-scented leaves and tender stems once the plant is knee-high and again before each bloom flush.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix, cover lightly, and keep moist for even germination of this slower purple-leaved type. Give it full sun and steady warmth to deepen the wine-purple leaf color, pinching tips to encourage branching. Harvest the aromatic purple foliage from midsummer on, cutting stems just above a leaf pair to spur regrowth.

planting.note: This vigorous perennial type benefits from an early indoor start before setting out after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth, barely covering it, and grow on to a sturdy transplant before setting out. This taller, clove-and-lemon scented species can become woody, so pinch early and give it room to bush out. Harvest leaves throughout the warm season; in frost-free areas it overwinters as a small shrub.

subcategory: adaptogen, tender perennial grown as annual

planting.note: A quick, early-flowering type that fruits fast even in shorter seasons.

directions: Sow indoors onto warm mix and lightly cover, transplanting once the weather settles. This fast, free-flowering strain sets seed readily and self-sows in mild gardens. Harvest the fragrant leaves early and often, and let a few plants flower to draw pollinators and save seed.

planting.note: Root cuttings in shallow water or saturated soil once temperatures warm.

directions: Push stem cuttings into mud at a pond margin or in a container standing in a saucer of water. This succulent creeper thrives in constant wetness and full sun, trailing and rooting as it goes. Harvest the small paired leaves and tender stems anytime once the mat is established, and it rebounds quickly after cutting.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed and set plants out in cool spring conditions.

directions: Give seed several weeks of cold, moist chilling before sowing on the surface of gritty, fast-draining mix. This cold-loving alpine wants full sun, lean soil, and sharp drainage; it languishes in heat and humidity. Let plants grow undisturbed for four or five years, then lift the fragrant rose-scented roots in fall for harvest.

planting.note: Autumn-sow chilled seed in shaded, humus-rich forest soil.

directions: Sow stratified seed shallowly in cool, shaded, loose woodland soil rich in organic matter. Like its American cousin it germinates slowly and demands deep shade and even moisture through several growing seasons. Harvest the aromatic roots after five or six years in autumn once the tops yellow, saving berries to sow the next generation.

planting.note: Establish rooted cuttings or stratified seedlings in spring in a partly shaded spot.

directions: Set out rooted cuttings or seedlings in moist, well-drained soil at a woodland edge with morning sun and afternoon shade. This prickly, very cold-hardy shrub is slow to start but tough once established, needing little care beyond mulch. Dig portions of the root and root bark from plants three years or older in fall, leaving the crown to regrow.

planting.note: Plant potted vines in spring at the base of a sturdy trellis in partial shade.

directions: Set the vine in cool, rich, evenly moist soil with its roots shaded and its top free to climb toward light. Provide a strong trellis and grow both a male and female plant nearby for reliable fruit set. Pick the ruby clusters when fully red in late summer; the berries carry sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and pungent notes at once.

planting.note: Nick and soak seed, then direct-sow where the deep taproot can grow undisturbed.

directions: Scarify and soak the hard seed overnight, then sow a half inch deep in deep, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. Because it forms a long taproot it dislikes transplanting, so plant it in its permanent spot. Dig the long yellow-cored roots in fall from plants at least three or four years old, then slice and dry them.

planting.note: Start soaked seed early indoors; provide support for the climbing stems.

directions: Soak the seed a day, then sow warm and grow the seedlings on before setting them near a trellis. The wiry, feathery stems climb and the plant forms a cluster of tuberous roots below ground. Lift the finger-like roots after eighteen months to two years, replanting the crown to continue the clump.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then set young plants where they can twine on support.

directions: Chill the seed before surface-sowing, then grow the twining seedlings on and give them a trellis or shrub to climb. This gentle ginseng relative likes rich, moist, well-drained soil and light shade. Dig the sweet, spindle-shaped roots in autumn from plants two or three years old, then wash and dry them.

planting.note: A high-altitude crop needing cool days and cold-tolerant of light frost.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly in cool, loose, well-drained soil, thinning seedlings to give each root room. It naturally grows in cold highlands, so it needs a long, cool season and tolerates frost but not heat. Harvest the small radish-like storage roots after roughly seven to eight months, then cure them in the sun.

planting.note: Fresh seed needs light and a cool chill; germination is slow and uneven.

directions: Sow fresh seed on the surface, press it in for light, and be patient through slow, staggered germination. Grow plants in rich, moist soil with some shade; they regrow leaves quickly, hence the name tomorrow's leaf. Pick outer stalks and leaves regularly, and prevent flowering to keep the plant productive since it dies after it seeds.

planting.note: Surface-sow the fine seed and set plants out after the soil warms.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto moist mix without covering it and keep it lightly shaded until sprouts appear. Grow plants in rich, well-drained soil with light shade; they spread slowly by rhizome to form a low patch. Dig the plump storage roots in fall of the first or second year, replanting a few pieces to renew the stand.

planting.note: Root cuttings or divisions in spring on a trellis in a contained spot.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant divisions in rich, moist soil against a sturdy support, keeping it contained since it spreads. The twining vine grows quickly and forms swollen tuberous roots over several seasons. Dig the reddish-brown roots in fall from mature plants three or more years old, then slice and dry them.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted stalks after the soil is thoroughly warm in late spring.

directions: Start a fresh stalk by rooting a store-bought base in water, or divide an established clump in spring. Grow in rich, moist soil with full sun and feed regularly for lush blades. Harvest by cutting whole stalks at the base once the swollen bulbous ends are pencil-thick or more.

planting.note: Transplant after all frost danger; this heat-lover needs a long warm season.

directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then start indoors weeks before setting out in warm soil. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and space generously for the large bushy plants. Harvest the fleshy red calyces once they swell around the seed pods for the tart, crimson tea.

planting.note: Direct-sow after frost, as fennel resents root disturbance from transplanting.

directions: Sow seed directly where plants are to grow, since the taproot dislikes moving. Give it full sun and fertile soil, and keep it away from dill to prevent crossing. Let flowers set seed, then harvest the ripe aromatic seeds for a sweet anise-flavored tea.

planting.note: Start indoors or set out plants after frost for the bright chartreuse foliage.

directions: Surface-sow seed in warmth, as light and steady moisture aid germination. This selection keeps golden-green leaves best in full sun with average, well-drained soil. Pick the anise-scented foliage and lavender flower spikes through summer for herbal tea.

planting.note: A slow-to-bolt strain, so spring sowings hold leaf a little longer before setting seed.

directions: Direct sow a half inch deep in cool soil and thin seedlings to give each plant room to branch. This selection is prized for slower bolting, but for seed harvest simply allow it to flower on schedule. Gather the umbels once the seed heads dry to a light brown and rattle when shaken.

planting.note: One of the slowest bolting types, useful where a long cutting window matters.

directions: Scatter seed a half inch deep in well drained soil and keep evenly moist during germination. Though bred to resist bolting, it will still throw flower stalks in warm weather, which is when you steer it toward seed production. Harvest the dry tan fruits before the heads shatter and store them whole.

planting.note: A dependable slow-bolt variety that also finishes a reliable seed crop.

directions: Sow directly about a half inch deep in a sunny bed once frost danger eases. Water steadily until the plants establish, then let them bolt and set their round green fruits. When the seed heads dry to brown, clip and hang them over a cloth to catch falling seed.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool spring soil where the plants are to bloom and pod.

directions: Sow the black seeds shallowly, about a quarter inch deep, directly in the garden since the plants resent transplanting. After the pale flowers fade they form inflated, horned seedpods that ripen from green to papery tan. Snip the dry pods and shake out the peppery black seeds used in flatbreads and pickles.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in a warm, sunny spot for a summer seed crop.

directions: Direct sow a half inch deep in warm soil and keep lightly moist until the seedlings appear. This legume carries clusters of blue clover-like flowers that mature into small pods. The dried green-brown seed and ground seedpods flavor traditional Alpine and Georgian cheeses and breads.

planting.note: A tall heavy-heading strain grown mainly for pickling seed.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and thin generously, since this variety grows tall and benefits from a sheltered spot. It is selected for large seed umbels, making it a classic choice for dill pickles. Harvest the whole flowering heads as the seed turns from green to brown.

planting.note: A leafy, aromatic strain that is slow to bolt but still sets good seed.

directions: Sow directly a quarter inch deep and keep the soil evenly moist for germination. This selection is prized for abundant, richly flavored foliage, yet allowed to flower it produces plenty of seed. Cut the umbels when the seeds ripen to brown and dry them before storing.

planting.note: A compact, container-friendly dwarf dill that still flowers and seeds.

directions: Direct sow shallowly or grow in a deep pot, thinning to a few sturdy plants. This dwarf, slow-bolting form stays tidy and rarely needs staking. When you want seed, let it bloom and gather the small umbels as they dry to tan.

planting.note: Sow early in cool soil so seed pods fill before summer heat.

directions: Direct sow a quarter to half inch deep in cool spring soil and thin the fast-growing seedlings. The tall, branching plants bloom bright yellow, then form slender pods packed with pungent dark seed. Cut whole plants as the lowest pods dry, then thresh before the pods shatter and scatter seed.

planting.note: Slow biennial started early indoors; seed comes in the second season.

directions: Surface sow the fine seed indoors and keep it warm and moist, as germination is slow and light-dependent. Transplant into rich, consistently damp soil after frost, growing plants on through the season. Left to overwinter where hardy, they bolt the second year and yield the tiny, intense seeds used in spice blends.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out young plants once hard frost has passed.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it just moist until sprouts appear, then grow on in bright light. Set plants in sharply drained, lean soil and shear the whole mound back hard in early spring to keep it dense. Button-like yellow flowers rise above the silver foliage in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a hot, well-drained spot.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty mix and provide warmth and light for even sprouting. Give this green-leaved form full sun and dry, poor soil, cutting it back by half after bloom to prevent it from splitting open. Bright yellow pincushion flowers open on wiry stems in summer.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after the last frost for tidy low edging.

directions: Root softwood cuttings in summer since this compact selection is grown from cuttings to stay uniform. Plant in fast-draining soil and clip lightly to hold a neat cushion for knot gardens and low hedges. Small yellow buttons appear in summer and can be sheared off to favor the silvery foliage.

planting.note: Divide or plant out after frost into gritty, lean soil.

directions: Grow from division or cuttings to keep this selection true, and avoid rich or wet soil that makes it flop. Shear the cushion back after it opens up in midsummer heat to force fresh, feathery silver growth. The threadlike foliage forms a soft dome that is the whole point, so any flowers can be trimmed away.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; expect rhizomatous spread.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed under light and thin seedlings before transplanting. Plant in dry, well-drained ground and be aware that creeping roots can travel, so give it room or a buried barrier. The silvery-white aromatic foliage is cut for fresh and dried arrangements through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; wear gloves when handling.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep warm until germination, then grow seedlings in bright light. Plant in lean, well-drained soil and shear back in spring to maintain the mounded blue-green foliage. Handle with care in sun, as the sap can cause a phototoxic skin rash; dull yellow flowers appear in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; propagate vegetatively for best color.

directions: Take cuttings to keep the intense steel-blue foliage of this compact selection consistent. Grow in poor, sharply drained soil and clip in spring to hold a tidy dome. Wear gloves to avoid skin irritation from the sap, and expect small mustard-yellow flowers in summer.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep in poor to average soil for the best flowering. This bushy strain is prized for its cream-marbled variegated foliage that stays showy even between blooms. Snip the bright flowers for salads once they are fully open.

planting.note: Start indoors weeks ahead and set out while weather is still cool.

directions: Sow into flats and keep the seeds cool and dark until they germinate. Transplant into rich, moist soil for a display that peaks in spring and fall. Pinch spent blooms often and pick the large flat flowers to decorate plates and cakes.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring; this cultivar is not grown from seed.

directions: Plant divisions in rich, moist soil with room for good airflow around each clump. This selection is valued for large true-red flowers and strong resistance to powdery mildew. Deadhead spent heads to prolong bloom and harvest the edible florets for garnishes.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring; this hybrid is propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant in full sun with sharp drainage, as soggy winter soil is the main cause of loss. This sturdy hybrid produces dense, powdery-blue bottlebrush spikes that swarm with pollinators. Deadhead lightly and harvest leaves and edible flowers for tea through the season.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of the last frost or direct sow once soil warms.

directions: Cover the slender seeds lightly and keep warm until they sprout in a week or so. These lacy-leaved plants form tidy mounds smothered in small single flowers all summer. The bright lemon-yellow blooms have a citrusy tarragon flavor; pick them fresh for salads.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings outdoors after frost danger has passed.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and barely cover it, keeping the soil warm and moist for even sprouting. The deep purple-red leaves color best in full to partial sun, so give them bright light. Pick leaves regularly and pinch off flower spikes to keep the plant productive and the foliage tender.

planting.note: Start indoors early and plant out when nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface and keep it bright and moist until sprouts appear, as buried seed germinates poorly. This broad-leaved type grows large flat leaves prized for wrapping, so feed lightly and water steadily. Harvest the biggest lower leaves first and remove flower stalks to prolong the season.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after all frost danger passes.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm soil and keep it moist until the seedlings appear. Pinch out the growing tips once plants have several sets of leaves to build a full, branching plant with its signature purple stems and anise scent. Harvest sprigs regularly and remove flower buds to keep the leaves coming.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Surface-sow and press the seed in lightly, keeping it warm and bright for germination. This selection shows green tops with striking purple undersides and a warm cinnamon-like scent. Pinch tips to keep it bushy and harvest leaves from the top, removing flower spikes as they appear.

planting.note: Transplant out once the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. Pinch early to build a bushy plant of this citrus-scented basil used in Thai and Lao dishes. Harvest leaves young and pinch off flower spikes to keep the plant leafy and productive.

planting.note: Set out hardened seedlings once the danger of hard frost has passed.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of gritty, well-drained soil and press in without covering, as light aids germination. Space plants to knit into a low mat and shear lightly after the summer flush of pink flowers. Snip sprigs anytime for cooking, with the strongest flavor just as the blooms open.

planting.note: Plant out into warm, freely draining ground once frosts are finished.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and grow the seedlings hard in gritty compost to keep them compact. Give this thyme full baking sun and stony, alkaline soil, watering only when the ground is bone dry. Clip narrow, aromatic leaves for the kitchen and cut back after the small white-to-pink flowers fade.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after the last spring frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Propagate from softwood cuttings and plant into lean, gritty ground with the crown just at soil level. This is the sweet, low-camphor French cooking thyme, so keep it in full sun and pinch tips to build a bushy plant. Harvest sprigs freely and shear the plant after its pale lilac summer bloom to prevent it going woody.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a sunny, sharply drained pocket or edging.

directions: Grow this tidy, dome-shaped selection from cuttings and space it closely for a neat kitchen-garden edge. Its dense habit needs excellent drainage and full sun to stay firm and aromatic. Trim regularly for cooking and give a hard shear after the small pale flowers to keep the cushion compact.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost in a warm, open, free-draining site.

directions: Root cuttings and set out this stiffly upright form, which makes tidy little bushes ideal for harvesting. Give it poor, gritty soil and full sun, avoiding rich feeding that softens growth. Cut stems for the kitchen throughout the season and clip over after the mauve flowers to refresh the plant.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into gritty soil so they establish before summer heat.

directions: Increase this hairy-leaved mat-former by division or rooted pieces and press it firmly onto free-draining ground. It spreads into a soft grey-green carpet smothered in clear pink flowers in early summer. Shear off the spent blooms to tidy the mat, and use the aromatic foliage sparingly in the kitchen.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out in spring into sharply drained, sunny soil.

directions: Propagate this creeping variegated form from cuttings, as it does not come true from seed. Plant into gritty, poor soil in full sun to keep the cream-edged, silvery foliage bright. Trim lightly for the kitchen and clip after the pale flowers to encourage dense, low growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a hot, gritty, sheltered spot as it dislikes winter wet.

directions: Raise this neat grey-leaved bun from cuttings and grow it lean in a raised bed or trough with fast drainage. It forms a low mound topped with soft pink flowers in early summer. Give it maximum sun and minimal water, harvesting the fine aromatic leaves as needed.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost into open, well-drained ground.

directions: Grow this reliable, rounded culinary thyme from cuttings for uniform plants. It makes a leafy, vigorous mound with a full, sweet flavor prized for cooking. Plant in gritty soil and full sun, harvesting sprigs freely and shearing after the pale pink summer bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions or cuttings in spring into sunny, sharply drained soil.

directions: Propagate this low, cream-variegated thyme from cuttings to preserve its markings. Grow it in poor, gritty soil and full sun where the pale leaf edges show best. Trim for the kitchen and shear the mat after its pale pink flowers to keep it dense.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings after frost into a warm, free-draining bed.

directions: Root cuttings of this upright, grey-leaved thyme, which carries a distinctive citrus-orange scent. Plant into lean, gritty soil in full sun and pinch to keep it bushy. Harvest the fragrant foliage for cooking and clip over after the pale flowers to prevent legginess.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Grow this gold-centered thyme from cuttings to keep its yellow-splashed leaves true. Give it full sun and gritty soil so the variegation stays vivid and the plant stays compact. Snip the mildly lemon-scented sprigs for cooking and shear lightly after the pale pink bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into gritty soil for a low ground-cover mat.

directions: Divide established plants or root pieces of this flat creeping thyme and press them onto free-draining ground. It spreads into a tight carpet covered with pale rose-pink flowers in summer, tough enough for light foot traffic. Shear off spent blooms and use the aromatic leaves in cooking as wanted.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sunny, sharply drained soil to establish before heat.

directions: Increase this creeping selection by division and firm it into gritty, lean ground in full sun. It forms a dense green mat lit up with deep purple-pink flowers in early summer. Clip after flowering to keep the carpet tidy and harvest sprigs for the kitchen as needed.

planting.note: Plant in spring into fast-draining soil where it can knit into a mat.

directions: Propagate this vigorous flat creeper by division and press pieces onto gritty, poor soil in full sun. It forms a very dense, glossy green carpet studded with rose-pink flowers and tolerates light traffic between pavers. Shear after bloom and clip aromatic sprigs for cooking whenever needed.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost into gritty, sunny ground.

directions: Grow this low, spreading thyme from cuttings to keep its narrow, needle-like foliage true. The leaves carry a warm orange-citrus scent that flavors poultry and marinades well. Plant in lean soil and full sun, harvesting freely and trimming after the pale pink flowers.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a hot, sharply drained spot; protect from winter wet.

directions: Start this Portuguese species from seed sown warm and grow it hard for compact plants. Its broad, resinous leaves carry a strong camphor-pine scent and it demands lean, gritty soil and full sun. Give it excellent drainage, harvest leaves sparingly, and cut back after the pink-purple flowering heads.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into a gritty trough or raised bed with full sun.

directions: Surface-sow this tiny Azorean thyme and prick out seedlings into sharply drained, gritty compost. It forms low, moss-like green tufts that carry a pine-and-tangerine scent when brushed. Grow it lean in full sun with minimal water and clip the small pink-lilac flowers after they fade.

planting.note: Set out seedlings after hard frost into open, well-drained ground.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of gritty compost and grow this East Asian species cool and lean. It creeps into a low mat of aromatic leaves topped with rosy-purple flowers in summer. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage, harvest sprigs for cooking, and shear after bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into gritty soil for a very low carpet.

directions: Increase this dwarf thyme by division and press the tiny rooted pieces onto lean, free-draining ground. It hugs the soil with minute leaves and pale pink flowers, ideal for crevices and between stepping stones. Grow it in full sun with little water and trim spent flowers to keep it neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sunny, sharply drained soil for a low mat.

directions: Divide or root pieces of this flat creeping thyme, whose leaves release a warm, nutmeg-like scent. Press plants onto gritty, poor ground in full sun where they knit into a tough green carpet with pale pink flowers. Shear after bloom and use the fragrant foliage to flavor cooking.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost into a bright, free-draining spot.

directions: Grow this bright chartreuse thyme from cuttings to keep its vivid lime-green foliage true. Give it full sun and gritty soil, where the fresh citrus-lime scent and leaf color are strongest. Snip sprigs for cooking, avoid rich soil, and shear over after the pale pink flowers.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings after frost into lean, sunny, well-drained soil.

directions: Propagate this upright culinary thyme from cuttings for consistent, bushy plants. It is grown for its full, robust flavor and tidy habit that stands up well to regular picking. Plant in gritty soil and full sun, harvest sprigs freely, and shear after the pale flowers to keep it from going woody.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost into gritty, full-sun ground.

directions: Grow this dense, mounded thyme from cuttings for uniform, cushion-shaped plants. Its finely textured, curly foliage stays neat and carries a good savory flavor for the kitchen. Plant in lean soil and full sun, harvest often, and clip lightly after the pale pink flowers to keep the mound tidy.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants only after nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: This vigorous, downy-mildew-resistant Genovese type is sold as vegetative plants because it is nearly sterile and rarely flowers. Space plants generously in warm, rich soil and pinch the tips often to build a bushy plant. Harvest leaves continuously through the season, taking the upper stems to keep new growth coming.

planting.note: Transplant cuttings once the soil is thoroughly warm in late spring.

directions: Grown for its striking cream-edged green leaves, this columnar basil is non-flowering and must be started from cuttings. Give it full sun and steady moisture, and shear lightly to maintain the upright, hedge-like habit. Pick young leaves for cooking and it also makes a handsome container plant.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about a month before the last frost and set out afterward.

directions: This award-winning dwarf Genovese forms a tidy, rounded mound perfect for pots and small beds. Sow seed shallowly on warm soil and thin to one strong seedling per cell. Pinch the growing tips regularly to delay bloom and keep the sweet, small leaves coming all summer.

planting.note: Begin indoors in warmth and move out after frost has passed.

directions: A naturally spherical small-leaved basil that grows into a dense green ball needing almost no shearing. Press seed lightly into warm mix and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. Snip leaves as needed and remove any stray flower buds to hold the neat shape.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors, then plant out into warm soil.

directions: Prized for its deep purple-black, slightly ruffled foliage that holds color better than many purple types. Surface-sow onto warm mix and give bright light so the pigment develops fully. Harvest leaves for color and mild clove flavor, pinching flowers to prolong the leafy phase.

planting.note: Start warm indoors and transplant after the danger of frost.

directions: A uniform dark purple basil bred for consistent color and full, rounded plants. Sow shallow on warm soil and grow in strong sun for the richest leaf tone. Cut leaves regularly for kitchen use and to encourage bushy branching.

planting.note: Sow indoors ahead of the last frost and set out into warm ground.

directions: A high-yielding Genovese-style basil selected for downy-mildew tolerance and slow bolting. Press seed lightly into warm mix and keep moist until germination. Harvest by cutting whole stems above a leaf pair to trigger vigorous regrowth for repeated cuttings.

planting.note: Start indoors and move outdoors after frost when soil is warm.

directions: One of the first basils bred with resistance to downy mildew while keeping classic Genovese aroma and flavor. Sow shallowly on warm mix and grow on in bright light. Pinch tips early and harvest stems often to maintain leafy, disease-resistant plants.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant once nights are warm.

directions: A Rutgers-bred sweet basil with strong downy-mildew resistance and large, aromatic leaves for pesto. Surface-sow on warm soil and keep evenly moist until sprouting. Cut whole stems for harvest to keep the plant branching and productive into late summer.

planting.note: Begin indoors before frost and set out into warm, rich soil.

directions: Another Rutgers release combining Italian sweet-basil flavor with high resistance to downy mildew. Press seed lightly into warm starting mix and grow under bright light. Harvest regularly by taking the top stems, which keeps plants dense and delays flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: A columnar, very slow-bolting Thai basil with anise flavor and an upright, well-branched habit. Sow shallow on warm mix and grow in full sun for the best licorice aroma. Its late flowering means long harvests of clean leaves with little pinching required.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and plant out once soil is dependably warm.

directions: A uniform Thai basil with purple stems, purple flower spikes, and a strong anise-licorice flavor for Southeast Asian cooking. Press seed lightly onto warm mix and keep moist to germinate. Harvest young stems often, though the ornamental flower spikes are attractive if left to bloom.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set out plants after frost danger passes.

directions: A traditional Genovese-type basil valued for classic pesto aroma and large, cupped green leaves. Sow shallow on warm mix and grow under strong light for stocky transplants. Cut whole stems above a node to harvest and keep the plant producing fresh growth.

planting.note: Raise indoors before the frost date and transplant into warm soil.

directions: A dependable field and greenhouse Genovese basil bred for uniformity and cold-tolerance early in the season. Press seed lightly into warm mix and keep evenly moist. Harvest stems frequently to encourage branching and to delay the onset of flowering.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and move plants outside after frost.

directions: A large-leaved Genovese basil with a robust aroma and good yields for fresh cutting. Surface-sow onto warm soil and grow on in bright light until ready to transplant. Take whole stems when harvesting to keep the plant compact and productive.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost into pots or beds.

directions: A very small-leaved dwarf basil that forms a dense little mound ideal for edging and containers. Press seed lightly into warm mix and thin to the strongest seedlings. Trim lightly for kitchen use and remove flower buds to keep the compact shape.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once nights are warm and settled.

directions: Also sold as Greek Columnar or Aussie Sweetie, this near-sterile hybrid rarely flowers and is grown from cuttings. It forms a tall, upright, shrubby plant with spicy sweet leaves and can be overwintered indoors. Give it full sun and shear as needed for a long harvest and a tidy column.

planting.note: Begin indoors and transplant out after the last frost.

directions: A dense, small-leaved basil that resembles a clipped boxwood and works well in ornamental plantings and pots. Sow seed shallowly on warm mix and grow in strong light. Harvest small leaves as needed and its naturally rounded form needs little shaping.

planting.note: Raise indoors early and transplant once soil warms.

directions: A compact Genovese-flavored basil bred for uniform plants and high yield in small spaces and containers. Press seed lightly into warm mix and keep evenly moist. Cut stems regularly for harvest to keep the plant bushy and slow to bolt.

planting.note: Sow indoors and set out after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: A classic Italian Genovese basil famed for its exceptionally fragrant, glossy green leaves. Surface-sow onto warm mix and keep moist until seedlings emerge. Harvest by cutting whole stems, and pinch out flower spikes to keep the intense aroma at its peak.

planting.note: Plant at pond margins or in wet soil in spring.

directions: A native wetland mint that thrives in saturated soil and at the edges of ponds and streams. Grow from divisions or transplants set into wet ground, where it spreads by rooting runners. Its lilac flower heads attract many pollinators, and leaves can be used for tea.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in moist, rich soil.

directions: A soft, fuzzy-leaved mint traditionally used for tea, with a mild, sweet spearmint-like flavor. Propagate from divisions or cuttings and grow in moist, fertile soil. Harvest leafy stems through the season and cut back before flowering to renew tender growth.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring in moist ground.

directions: A tall peppermint selection with blue-green foliage and a strong, clean menthol aroma prized for tea. As a piperita type it is sterile, so start it from cuttings or divisions. Keep it well watered, contain the runners, and harvest before flowering for peak flavor.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring.

directions: A hybrid of spearmint and corn mint with a sweet, gum-like flavor blending both parents. Grow from cuttings or divisions in moist, rich soil, keeping the runners in check. Harvest stems regularly and cut back before bloom to maintain tender foliage.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring in moist soil.

directions: A vigorous mint favored for mint juleps, with a milder, sweeter flavor than standard peppermint. Propagate from cuttings or divisions and grow in consistently moist, fertile soil. Contain the spreading roots and pick leafy stems throughout the season for drinks and garnishes.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring; grow vegetatively to keep the strain true.

directions: A robust spearmint selection with large, deep green leaves and a strong, sweet mint flavor. Propagate by division or cuttings to keep the plant uniform, and grow in moist, fertile soil. Harvest generously and shear before flowering to keep new leaves coming.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, rich soil.

directions: A red-stemmed spearmint hybrid grown commercially for oil and prized for its bright, sweet flavor. Propagate from divisions or cuttings and plant into consistently moist soil. Keep the spreading runners in bounds and harvest leafy stems before the plant blooms.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a hot, open, well-drained spot.

directions: Give it lean soil and full exposure; overly rich or wet ground shortens its life. Fat, pale pink buds open to soft lilac-white blooms with a sweet fragrance prized for baking. Cut spikes in early morning as color shows, and trim the whole plant back by a third once flowering finishes.

planting.note: Start seed early indoors so it blooms the first summer.

directions: Unusual among lavenders for flowering the first year from seed, this dwarf strain is easy to raise as an annual or perennial. Surface-sow onto warm, moist mix and leave uncovered, since light speeds sprouting. After hardening off, transplant to full sun and harvest the fragrant lavender-blue spikes through summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring into a hot, well-drained location.

directions: Notable for unusually dark blue-violet flowers on a lavandin, giving deep color for cutting and drying. Plant in full sun and fast-draining soil, keeping moisture away from the crown. Harvest spikes as they open for the richest color, and prune each spring to keep the bush full.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall so winter chill triggers spring germination.

directions: Sow only fresh seed on the surface in autumn, as it loses viability fast and needs cold and light. This stately biennial or short-lived perennial wants deep, moist, humus-rich soil in part shade. Harvest young stems and leaf stalks in the first year, and dig roots before the plant flowers and dies.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring into deep, loose ground the roots can travel through.

directions: Nick and soak the hard seed before sowing to speed sprouting, or start from root divisions. Give it deep, sandy, well-drained soil and a permanent spot, as the running roots resent disturbance. Dig the sweet taproots and runners in autumn of the third or fourth year, when they have thickened enough to harvest.

planting.note: Plant root divisions in spring into loose, fertile soil.

directions: Increase plants by dividing the fleshy roots or by surface-sowing the fine seed in warmth. Grow in rich, well-drained soil in sun to light shade, keeping it evenly moist during growth. The tubular rose-purple flowers open in late spring; lift the swollen roots in autumn of the first or second year.

planting.note: Sow in spring; plants often self-sow once established.

directions: Sow the small seed shallowly in ordinary garden soil and thin the seedlings once they establish. Grow in full sun with average moisture; the plant is undemanding and vigorous. Cut the leafy flowering tops in summer as the whorled pink blooms open along the square stems.

planting.note: Set out spring seedlings into deep, well-drained soil for the taproot.

directions: Chill the seed briefly, then sow shallowly and be patient, as germination is uneven. Grow in full sun in deep, loose soil so the taproot can develop, watering only moderately. The airy yellow-green umbels appear in summer; harvest the roots in autumn of the second or third year.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; site it where its spreading roots can be contained.

directions: Divide an established clump in spring, or surface-sow the dust-fine seed in warmth. Grow in full sun and ordinary to poor soil, and give it room, since the runners spread readily. Cut the silvery, aromatic leaves in early summer before flowering, when their oils are at their peak.

planting.note: Set out seedlings in spring; give the tall plant plenty of room.

directions: Surface-sow the extremely fine seed and press it in without covering, keeping the mix lightly moist. Grow in full sun in average, well-drained soil; the fast-growing plant reaches head height by late summer. Harvest the fern-like foliage just before the small flowers open in late summer, when leaf potency is highest.

planting.note: Set out spring seedlings into loose, well-drained soil for good root growth.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm mix, or divide an established clump in spring. Grow in sun to light shade in deep, fertile, well-drained soil that lets the red roots develop. Enjoy the violet-blue summer flowers, then lift the brick-red roots in autumn of the first or second year.

common_name: Andrographis (King of Bitters, Chuan Xin Lian)

planting.note: Transplant only into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Soak seed briefly, sow shallowly, and keep the mix warm for even sprouting. Grow in fertile, moist but well-drained soil with light shade in the hottest climates. Cut the intensely bitter leafy stems as the small white flowers begin to open, generally three to four months from planting.

planting.note: Sow in warm, damp ground; it thrives in wet, low-lying spots.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the surface of moist, rich soil and keep it constantly damp. Grow in full sun to light shade in wet ground, as it naturally colonizes ditches and paddy edges. Harvest the sprawling stems and small white daisy heads through the warm season as they mature.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost into well-drained soil.

directions: Root a stem cutting easily in moist mix, or start from seed in warmth. Grow in full sun to part shade in sandy, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering. The aromatic tuberous roots, the harvested part, develop over about six to seven months and are lifted at season's end.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in humus-rich shade.

directions: Increase clumps most reliably by dividing the rhizomes, as seed is slow and needs stratifying. Grow in part to full shade in cool, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil. The delicate spurred flowers open in spring above the heart-shaped leaflets; leaves are harvested in the growing season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as a grassy groundcover.

directions: Split established grassy clumps into tufts and replant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil. Grow in part shade to full sun and keep evenly watered until the groundcover knits together. The small tuberous root swellings, the medicinal part, are dug from mature two-year clumps.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of frost or set out young plants in spring.

directions: Sow seed lightly covered or plant transplants in sharply drained, gritty soil in full sun. Avoid overwatering, since this Mediterranean sub-shrub rots in wet ground. Harvest the gray-green leaves for a warming tea, gathering them before the plant flowers for the fullest flavor. Prune lightly each spring to keep the woody stems from getting leggy.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in a sunny, well-drained location.

directions: Bred for uniform silver mounds that shrug off summer humidity and winter cold better than most lavandins. Site it in full sun with excellent drainage and resist over-fertilizing, which softens the foliage. Enjoy the violet-blue flush in midsummer, cut spikes for fragrance, and shear the mound after bloom to keep it dense.

planting.note: Transplant into warm, gritty soil after frost.

directions: A robust lavandin noted for unusually broad silvery leaves and thick, oversized flower heads on sturdy stems. Plant in full sun with fast drainage and keep it on the dry side once rooted in. The large fragrant blooms are excellent for cutting; shear the plant back hard after flowering to maintain its rounded shape.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after the last spring frost.

directions: Distinctive for its soft pink-blushed flowers and plump buds on a compact silver mound. Give it full sun and gritty, freely draining soil, watering only until it settles in. Shear off spent flower stalks to encourage a lighter second flush, and prune lightly each spring above the woody base.

planting.note: Plant after frost; overwinter in a pot where hard freezes occur.

directions: A hybrid lavender with silvery, softly toothed foliage and a long season of deep violet-blue flowers. It tolerates heat and humidity better than most and blooms for months in mild regions. Plant in full sun with fast drainage, water lightly, and shear after each flush; move container plants under cover for winter in cold zones.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant upright rosemary; plant after frost in full sun.

directions: Also sold as Madalene Hill, this upright selection is valued for good winter hardiness and dependable pale-blue bloom. Plant in full sun with lean, well-drained soil and a sheltered aspect to buffer hard freezes. Cut fragrant sprigs for the kitchen year-round in mild areas, and prune after flowering to keep the shrub dense.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Bred for tall, straight, sturdy stems that make ready-made fragrant skewers for grilling. It grows strongly upright with narrow green leaves and blue flowers, forming a fine culinary hedge. Plant in full sun with lean, sharply drained soil, water infrequently, and cut the long stems as you need them.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a sunny, sharply drained site.

directions: A classic English upright rosemary with strong vertical stems and pale blue spring flowers, long favored for hedging. Plant in full sun with lean, well-drained soil and give it room to reach its full height. Harvest sprigs year-round in mild climates and prune after bloom to keep the tall growth tidy and clothed.

planting.note: Set out only after all frost has passed and nights are warm.

directions: Brushing the softly rounded leaves releases a spicy ginger-lemon fragrance, making it a favorite for containers and paths. Plant in rich but well-drained soil in full sun to light shade, and keep it evenly but not heavily watered. Pinch the stems often to promote bushy growth, and overwinter cuttings or whole plants indoors where frost occurs.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when soil and nights have warmed.

directions: Small, crinkled leaves give off a fruity strawberry-rose scent, and the plant carries dainty rosy-pink flowers. Grow it in a container of free-draining potting mix in full sun to part shade for easy overwintering. Pinch growing tips to keep it compact, let the soil dry slightly between waterings, and bring it indoors before the first frost.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a warm, sunny position.

directions: Finely cut, sticky leaves with a resinous pine-balsam scent give this species a feathery, aromatic texture. Grow it in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade and water moderately, allowing the surface to dry between drinks. Pinch to shape, enjoy the small pink flowers, and shelter it from frost by overwintering under cover.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out after frost.

directions: A low, spreading species whose foliage smells of coconut and tart gooseberry when crushed, carried on reddish trailing stems. It grows easily and even self-sows in mild climates, bearing tiny magenta flowers. Give it moist but well-drained soil in sun to part shade, and grow it as an annual or overwinter under glass where it freezes.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Named for its deeply dissected, lacy leaves, this rose-lemon scented geranium is popular for potpourri and flavoring. Grow it in free-draining soil in full sun to light shade, letting the mix dry slightly between waterings. Pinch regularly for a full plant, harvest leaves for the kitchen or crafts, and overwinter indoors away from frost.

planting.note: Transplant after frost when nights are reliably warm.

directions: This vigorous hybrid pairs cedar-and-eucalyptus scented foliage with unusually large, showy rose-pink flowers. Grow it in well-drained soil in full sun to part shade, watering moderately and feeding lightly for the best bloom. Pinch to keep it bushy, and take cuttings or lift the plant to overwinter under cover before frost.
